speaker: interviewer
text: December 1, 1996, interview with Mr. William Cohn. Location is Dallas, Texas, United States of America. Mr. Cohn, when we were last speaking, you were discussing your sister and her fiance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] She met-- my sister met him and dated him without my parents knowing. I think she was 17 then. And until finally she came out with it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But my parents didn't know yet that he wasn't Jewish. They hadn't met him yet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One time on a Sunday, and I remember it very well, he came to introduce himself to my parents. And that ceremony was performed in our best room, you know. And we had sliding doors that we-- three rooms. There were sliding doors. And I peeked through the sliding doors.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember him coming in. He was a typical German bureaucrat. He was tall. And he came with a top hat and a cutaway, you know? A-- this jacket that go like this, you know? They called it cutaway. And striped dark pants that he wore and with a top hat on a Sunday morning. And my sister introduced him to my parents and said, this is Paul Christian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Paul Christian. And so he spent the obligatory, I don't know, 10 or 15 minutes or so, you know? And then he left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my father, especially my father, said, Christian, he's not Jewish! No, of course not. Paul Christian.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my parents at first were [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] enraged about it. You know, a Jewish girl marrying a non-Jewish guy, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But gradually, he-- we got used to him. As I said, I also.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so-- but then, as I said, when Hitler came to power, he had to transfer to another city. And when I was in Italy on the training farm, they came and visited me. And they drove. He had a car. At that time, he had a car. And they drove in his car up to the German-Swiss border. And before they came to the border, my sister got out of the car and took the train over to the German-Swiss border. And the other side of the border, she joined her fiance again in the car. And they came and visited me in Italy. And they did the same thing on the way back home because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were not [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] allowed to have a relationship. A Jew and a Christ-- a non-Jew and a so-called Aryan were not allowed to have relationships, sexual relationships between men and women. And that was already against the law. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] just the funny thing about it is that he looked everything but German. He had bright black hair, straight, but very black, real black hair. And he had a long hooked nose like they say the Jews have. So he looked more Jewish than my sister did, or we did. But they went for six years together. But then, as I said, my parents [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] made my sister leave the country. And she got a visa, an immigration visa to England under the condition that she would work there as a house maid. And so then they broke up their engagement, because he was not willing to leave the country. Because he had his career in the government, and he was much too German in all his ways. For him to live in another country would just been very difficult. So they broke up. The engagement broke up. And so when my sister left the country, my parents brought her to the train station [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Berlin. And she wanted to kiss my parents goodbye, but the SS guards pulled her away and didn't let her kiss my parents goodbye. And she never saw them again. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And then soon after, my father died. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he had been in the concentration camp in Buchenwald. And that happened after the so-called Kristallnacht that all Jewish establishments were destroyed, and all Jewish males-- all Jewish males that they could get hold of-- were interned in concentration camps. And when my father came after, it was-- he wasn't walking then. He had a stroke. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] somehow he made his way home. But he was-- he was not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's like psychologically, he was also not normal anymore. He started screaming sometimes in the middle of the day, you know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister was still there at that time, when they-- our store was ransacked. And they came-- that's what I know from my sister, because I wasn't there anymore. I was in Palestine at the time. And my sister told me all of this. And they came at night, in the evening, and broke our big shop windows, you know, and threw all the merchandise out into the street. And then around four o'clock in the morning, they came up and told my sister and my father [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to pick up all the shards, the glass, with their bare hands, and threw things at them, shoes and boots at them. And then, later that day, my father was ordered to come to the police. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother tried to persuade him to run away, but he wouldn't. And so he went to the police, and with all the other Jews, he was interned in Buchenwald, and never came back. It was the beginning of his end, you know. And, by the way, that reminds me of-- my sister also told me something else, what my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One time there's some Nazi officers came up to our third-floor apartment and wanted to get into our apartment. And so, as they were drunk, and my mother pushed them down stairs. And they all fell down the stairs. My mother pushed them down. She was a very courageous woman. And she was very little, but very courageous woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: After she pushed him down the stairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, no. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yeah. There are other things I could. Somebody came and promised them-- when my father was in the concentration camp, somebody came and offered that he would get them out-- that he would get them out of concentration camp. And he asked for money, and they gave him money. And then he came back and just was here and they couldn't get work yet, but he will, and so on. And he always asked for more and more money. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] finally, they gave him all the silverware I think they had, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he just disappeared. But he also-- they were afraid they would blackmail him. Blackmail them. That they tried to get my father out.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I don't know who he was and what he was, whether he was a Nazi that just did that, or-- and they never knew what he was. But they were afraid of being blackmailed, you know? That was another incident then.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister went to England, to Scotland, and worked very hard in the household there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you left home?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old was I? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 21. 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you working at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working in that farm. First I was working under a false name in a forest, yeah, for 13 months. And then I had a fight with the forester and I left. And then I was sent with a Zionist organization to a tanning farm in northern Italy. And then I received a certificate of immigration for Palestine. And I went back to Germany for, what, two weeks or so to say goodbye to my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: If I can go back to your work in the forest, what kind of things were you doing in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were cutting wood, lumber. Timber. You know, lumber. Especially for mines [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] to be used for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] reinforcing the tunnels which were dug into the mines. And there was-- the younger trees were used for that. And then we would cut them in to the sizes, and cut the trees first, and shorten them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you left when you had a fight with a forester?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. He was a very unpleasant fellow. He was an alcoholic, also. But he was a very efficient forester. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we just didn't get along together. Until I finally came to-- [INAUDIBLE], couldn't take it anymore. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] For instance, I remember [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he had also a field, this was grain, you know. And I worked on a very hot summer day in the field. And when I came home to the house, I drank cold water. And apparently that upset Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. And then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] afterwards I worked in the stable. He had some pigs and cows, couple of cows. And I worked in the stable. And I was leaning against the stable, I guess, and suddenly I-- I lost conscious and I dropped. And when I woke up, I heard his laugh. He laughed at me. He laughs that I dropped unconscious.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was kind of where we-- or, he puts [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] traps out in the forest to catch foxes. Because he strapped-- they caught the fox in the leg, you know. And then you went out in the morning with a club and hit them over the head and took them home, and sold it to furriers. It was kind of [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: But he took me in knowing that I was a Jew. He took a chance, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you paid for the work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. On the contrary, my father paid him. For taking me in. That was shortly after I had to leave school, you know. It was of the best that I leave town, you know? That I disappear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there? I stayed home for a couple of months or so. And I traveled to Berlin a couple of times a week or so to a professor and studied painting, or drawing, for about four weeks. And then I got this appointment for the training farm, and I went to Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your professor's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walton Belk, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the training farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Training farm was in a very beautiful area of northern Italy, in [INAUDIBLE], which used to be Austria before the first world war. It was in the mountains of the Dolomites. Some beautiful mountains. And there was a farm with many vineyards, you know. And the vineyard-- that farm was run by a peasant. It belonged to a Jewish man. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he had given it to a farmer, to workers, you know, to a peasant. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were a group of about, I would say, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] maybe 10 or 12 young people. You know, girls and guys, all Jewish, yeah. All German except one. No, two. Yeah. One was from Lithuania. And the other one, yeah, came also from Germany, but he was from Eastern Europe, immigrated.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, and it was a very nice group of young people. And we all worked as this peasant taught us how to work, you know? We all came from a militarized bourgeois environment. We didn't know how to work with tools, and farming, agriculture, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I only had learned it in the forest, so I was [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] more [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] success. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was already the expert, so to say. And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, so it was a beautiful town. One of the happiest times I ever had in my life in Italy, there in northern Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you looking to learn a particular trade?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. My father thought that when he made this agreement with the forester that I would learn forestry, because, at that time, and also in later years, forests were planted very much in Palestine, and then later in Israel. Even today, you know. Because to improve the climate, and the soil. And so my father thought forestry would be a good profession there. But forestry in Germany and planting trees in Israel are quite a different story. I mean, there was no expertise in anything like that, you know. This was very different forestry. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, there was no lumbering there. We were happy if a tree grew, you know? They wouldn't cut a tree down. And there was no wildlife, you know? So it was very different. Would never be applicable. What I would learn there wouldn't be applicable over there. So what we were trained for, so we just work. You know, get used to work in the fields or wherever, you know? There were some training farms that were-- the people there were taught trades, yes, like brick laying, carpentry, machinist, and stuff like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there, that was specially just only agriculture, and particularly in vineyards. And we had-- there was a hillside that had been a bullet, and the trees had been cut down. Big trees, old trees. And we had to dig out the stumps, tree stumps. And there was an accident there afterwards, suffered from that for years. I dislocated my shoulder. I was building, you know, the [? high ?] [? logs. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then we wanted-- the tree was frozen, and we wanted to push it on the other side into the valley. And I pushed with my back, and my jacket got caught by one of the roots, and I was pulled with it. You know, like this. And then I jumped off and the stump [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] turned back, went back on me. And the [INAUDIBLE] dislocated the shoulder. And afterwards I have pain very often until it was operated on. I couldn't even use this arm anymore. So it was operated on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have surgery right after the accident?

speaker: interviewee
text: After what?

speaker: interviewer
text: After the accident, did you have surgery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no. In fact, I had surgery in the British army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that many years later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Many years, and about 24, 25 times I dislocated it. I couldn't use-- I couldn't hardly could use the arm anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, about 3/4 of a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to Germany for two weeks or so, and then to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you decide to leave Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Early on. In fact, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before Hitler came to power, there was a Jewish family that settled in my town which came from-- they came from Russia, from Odessa. And the whole family, they'd settled in our town, and they opened up a store, also. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And he started to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] teach Hebrew to whoever wanted, you know. And I went to learn Hebrew from him, just the beginning. And also, became a Zionist. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's a time, I think, when my father burned my sketch books. I think I told my father that we cannot live in this country anymore, you know? It's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as human beings, you know? We've been degraded, you know, and dishonored. And we-- I couldn't take it and we should leave. But at that time, nobody-- most people didn't know what would come. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] he thought that it would pass. It would be just an episode, an historical episode, and sooner or later, rather sooner than later, it would be overthrown, you know? And it would have been like a nightmare. And nobody expected it would last that slowly and become [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that excessive, you know? That extreme.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, see, Germany was a country that was used to law, to a law-abiding society. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they could not-- nobody could imagine that the government would become criminal and murderous. Just nobody could believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to leave the country legally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to obtain a passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to obtain a passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had to go to the ministry and get a release from the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the release state?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that I've been to an office. And there was an officer, an army officer. And he was very friendly. See, many of the army, they're not Nazis. They were just military people. But they were loyal Germans, but not Nazis. Or even not nationalistic, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll need to take a tape break.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
